metadata.seed: I feel like. I don't know. I don't really even deserve to be hear if I can't do well enough. I see all these other students who are doing well and doing better and want to be there. I don't know why I'm here, even at uni, to be honest.

patient.query: I feel like. I don't know. I don't really even deserve to be hear if I can't do well enough. I see all these other students who are doing well and doing better and want to be there. I don't know why I'm here, even at uni, to be honest.

automatic_thoughts: I don't deserve to be here
I don't know why I'm here

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of doubt about your place here. What evidence do you have that supports the idea that you don’t deserve to be at university? How might you view the situation if you considered that you might be here for reasons you haven’t yet fully recognized?

patient.query: I guess it just feels like everyone around me is so much better at this than I am. Like, I'm always second-guessing my work and my contributions. Sometimes, I think maybe I just slipped through the cracks and got lucky to even be here. It’s hard to imagine it any other way, though. I mean, what if they really did make a mistake?

triggers: thinking I slipped through the cracks
automatic_thoughts: I got lucky to be here

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling like you’re not good enough and that maybe you got lucky. What evidence do you have that supports that idea, and can you think of times when you’ve actually contributed successfully? How might you view those moments in a way that acknowledges your strengths?

patient.query: I don’t know… I guess sometimes I do get positive feedback, but I just brush it off like it doesn’t mean much. It’s more like I think they’re just being nice or trying to make me feel better. I remember a few times when I felt proud of what I did, but then I just go back to doubting myself and thinking I’m not really that capable. It's like I can't hold onto those good moments for too long.

automatic_thoughts: trying to make me feel better

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that people are just being nice when they give you positive feedback? Can you recall a specific moment when you felt genuinely proud and how that felt? What might happen if you let that feeling stay a bit longer before you start doubting yourself again?

patient.query: I mean, I guess I don’t have solid evidence... it just feels like it’s easier to believe they’re just being polite. I remember one time I worked really hard on a project, and my boss said I did a great job. I felt this spark of pride, like, "Wow, maybe I can do this." But then, almost right after, I thought about all the things I could’ve done better. If I tried to hold onto that proud moment, I don’t know

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught between feeling proud and then questioning whether you truly earned that praise. What evidence do you have that the compliment was genuine, and what evidence might suggest it was just politeness? How might you view the situation if you considered both possibilities at the same time?

patient.query: It’s just hard to hang onto those good feelings. I remember my boss saying I did a great job, and for a moment, I felt proud. But then I started thinking of all the ways I could've done it better. It’s like I get this brief spark of hope, and then I dive right back into doubt. That cycle just feels so exhausting sometimes.

triggers: boss saying I did a great job
automatic_thoughts: I dive right back into doubt

llm_response.response: It sounds like you get a quick burst of pride, only to feel that you could have done more. What evidence do you have that your work wasn’t already good enough? How might you let that moment of pride stay for a bit longer before the doubt starts?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like the pride fades quickly. I just keep looking at the little mistakes I made or the things I didn’t do perfectly. It’s like those little details just overshadow everything else. Maybe letting that moment of pride stay longer feels scary because then I’d have to accept that I did something well, and what if I don’t feel that way next time? It’s like, if I let myself believe it once, I’m afraid I’ll be disappointed again later.

automatic_thoughts: if I believe it once, I'll be disappointed again

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that feeling proud now might set you up for future disappointment. What experiences have led you to think that? Have there been times when you felt proud and it didn’t end in disappointment?

patient.query: I think a lot of it comes from... I don’t know, times when I thought I was doing well and then I stumbled. Like in school, I’d get good grades sometimes, but then I’d bomb a test or miss a deadline, and it just reminded me that I’m not as good as I thought. It’s like I’m always waiting for the other shoe to drop. There have been a couple of times when I felt proud and it didn’t end in disappointment, but those

automatic_thoughts: I'm not as good as I thought
I'm always waiting for the other shoe to drop

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve noticed a pattern where a good performance is followed by a setback, and that makes you feel uncertain about your abilities. What was different about the moments when you felt proud and didn’t end up disappointed? How might you use those experiences to reframe what “doing well” looks like for you?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I can see that pattern. Those moments when I felt proud felt… more genuine? Like, maybe I was in a good headspace or just focused on what I was doing without overthinking it. I can’t really pinpoint what was different, but maybe there was less pressure. But reframing what “doing well” looks like? That feels tricky. I mean, part of me wants to think it could just be about doing my best in the moment, but then another part

automatic_thoughts: doing my best in the moment

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a shift when you feel proud—maybe the pressure eases and you’re more present. What does “doing well” look like for you right now, and how do you tell when you’re truly doing your best? Could there be moments or clues that help you decide which of those perspectives feels more accurate in a given situation?

patient.query: I guess “doing well” for me usually means meeting expectations or avoiding mistakes, you know? But maybe that’s not fair to myself. Sometimes I can feel it when I’m really engaged and just losing track of time, like when I’m in a flow. But then, I quickly second-guess myself, thinking, “Was that really good enough?” It just feels easier to fall back into that critical mindset. I don’t know, I think I need to remind myself that it’s okay

automatic_thoughts: It's easier to fall back into a critical mindset.

llm_response.response: When you’re in a flow state, what does that experience feel like beyond the idea of meeting expectations? What evidence do you have that the process itself can be enough, even if you think a mistake might have slipped in? How might you reframe the question “Was that good enough?” to include the value of the effort and engagement you’re putting in?

patient.query: Um, when I’m in that flow state, it feels… freeing? Like I’m really absorbed in what I’m doing, and the worries just fade for a bit. But then afterward, I get scared because I know it’s temporary, and I panic about whether I can get back there again. I guess if I think about it, I can see how the effort itself does count for something, even if there were mistakes. It’s just hard to shake that fear of not being “good

automatic_thoughts: can I get back there again?
the effort itself does count for something

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a mix of relief during flow and then worry afterward. What evidence do you have that the flow experience is truly temporary? How might you view the effort you put in, even when mistakes happen?
